welcome.heading: Welcome to NVEIL AI !
welcome.intro: NVEIL is a data visualization platform. Upload your own data, describe what you want to see, and our AI creates real, interactive visualizations from your actual numbers — not static images.
welcome.privacy: Your trust is our top priority. Your data is never used to train our models. Everything remains private, and every result is transparent to ensure you stay in control.
welcome.feedback: A sample Airbnb dataset is pre-loaded so you can see NVEIL in action right away. Take the tour or dive straight into the chat!
welcome.thanks: Thank you for being part of the adventure. We can't wait to hear from you!

explore.heroSubtitle: Discover interactive visualizations, powerful data transforms, and color palettes — all generated from your data with a simple prompt.
explore.heroLibraries: Powered by Plotly, deck.gl, VTK, force-graph, Kedro, and more
explore.vizTypesSubtitle: Every type is fully tweakable and multiple visualizations can be combined together. Many are available in both 2D and 3D variants.
explore.moreCardDesc: These are just a few examples. Every visualization is hand-crafted with precision — describe what you need and NVEIL will build it from your data.
explore.toolkitShowcaseDesc: Behind every visualization, NVEIL orchestrates intelligent data pipelines powered by Choregraph (built on Kedro). Transforms are automatically selected and chained based on your intent — filtering, aggregating, geocoding, time-series forecasting, and 50+ operations. Each step is auditable: inspect intermediate datasets, trace lineage, and understand exactly how your data was shaped before rendering.
explore.barPrompt: Show revenue by product category, sorted descending
explore.barAlt: Also known as: column chart, bar graph
explore.lineShort: Reveal trends over time with connected series.
explore.linePrompt: Plot monthly sales for the last 24 months
explore.lineAlt: Also known as: time series, trend line, line graph
explore.pointShort: Spot correlations and clusters in (x, y) space.
explore.pointPrompt: Scatter plot of Age vs Income, color by Region
explore.pointAlt: Also known as: scatter plot, XY plot, dot plot, scattergram
explore.histogramShort: See the shape of a distribution at a glance.
explore.histogramPrompt: Histogram of exam scores with 30 bins
explore.histogramAlt: Also known as: frequency chart, distribution plot
explore.boxPrompt: Box plots of salary by department
explore.boxAlt: Also known as: box-and-whisker plot, box plot
explore.sectorShort: Show parts of a whole as proportional slices.
explore.sectorPrompt: Pie chart of market share by vendor
explore.sectorAlt: Also known as: pie chart, donut chart, ring chart
explore.violinShort: Combine density estimation with box plot summaries.
explore.violinPrompt: Violin plot of salary distribution by department
explore.violinAlt: Also known as: density plot, KDE plot, beanplot
explore.candleShort: Track price movements with open-high-low-close bars.
explore.candlePrompt: Candlestick chart of stock prices over 3 months
explore.candleAlt: Also known as: candlestick chart, OHLC chart, Japanese candlestick
explore.surfacePrompt: Generate a smooth elevation surface from (x,y,z)
explore.surfaceAlt: Also known as: 3D surface, mesh surface, terrain plot
explore.unigridAlt: Also known as: heatmap, heat map, density matrix
explore.contourPrompt: Contour plot of elevation data with filled regions
explore.contourAlt: Also known as: iso-line plot, level curve, topographic map
explore.mapShort: Heatmaps, scatter points, choropleths, and more on interactive maps.
explore.mapPrompt: Choropleth map of population density by country
explore.mapAlt: Also known as: choropleth, geo map, thematic map
explore.graphPrompt: Build a collaboration graph colored by department
explore.graphAlt: Also known as: network graph, node-link diagram, social network
explore.sankeyShort: Visualize flows and quantities between stages.
explore.sankeyPrompt: Sankey diagram of energy production to consumption
explore.sankeyAlt: Also known as: flow diagram, alluvial diagram
explore.parallelShort: Compare many dimensions on parallel axes.
explore.parallelPrompt: Parallel coordinates of car specs colored by MPG
explore.parallelAlt: Also known as: parallel coordinates, multivariate plot
explore.partitionShort: Show hierarchical data as nested rectangles.
explore.partitionPrompt: Treemap of company headcount by department and team
explore.partitionAlt: Also known as: treemap, sunburst, hierarchical chart
explore.vectorShort: Arrows and streamlines showing direction and magnitude.
explore.vectorPrompt: Show wind velocity as a vector field with streamlines
explore.vectorAlt: Also known as: quiver plot, flow field, velocity field, wind map
explore.voxelShort: Render 3D scalar fields with transfer functions.
explore.voxelPrompt: Render a CT volume emphasizing bone structures
explore.voxelAlt: Also known as: volume rendering, 3D voxel, volumetric
explore.dataToolkitSubtitle: 50+ data transforms you can invoke by simply describing what you need. The AI selects and chains them automatically.
explore.PointNote:
Displays observations as points in (x, y) to reveal correlation, clusters, and outliers. Extra variables can drive color, size, or shape to add context.
Try: “Show a scatter plot of Age vs Income; color by Region and size by BodyWeight.”
explore.GraphNote:
Represents relationships as a node–link network with layouts (force-directed, radial, hierarchical). Useful for communities, centrality, and paths.
Try: “Build a collaboration graph: nodes are employees, links connect people who worked on the same project; color nodes by department.”
explore.LineNote:
Connects ordered samples to show trends over time or sequence; supports multi-series, bands, and smoothing.
Try: “Plot monthly Sales for the last 24 months and add a 3-month moving average.”
explore.UnigridNote:
Projects scalar values onto a regular grid; cell color encodes magnitude. For scattered inputs, values are interpolated to the grid with a Gaussian kernel; Resolution controls grid density.
Try: “Create a heatmap of surface temperature over longitude/latitude with a high resolution grid.”
explore.SurfaceNote:
Transforms scattered data into a continuous representation. In 2D, a smoothing spline yields a filled area that balances fidelity and noise. In 3D, values are estimated on a regular grid via Gaussian Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression; Smoothing Intensity tunes kernel width and Resolution sets grid density.
Try: “Generate a smooth elevation surface from (x,y,z) samples with smoothing=0.4 and a medium grid resolution.”
explore.VolumeRenderingNote:
Performs direct volume rendering of 3D scalar fields using a transfer function for color/opacity. Adjust window level/width for contrast, and clip or resample as needed.
Try: “Render a CT volume and emphasize bone by centering around HU≈300 with a narrow window.”
explore.TextMarkNote:
Presents one or more KPI cards rather than a chart — best for headline values, categorical summaries, or labels that should stand on their own. Use when a chart would obscure a simple, quantified headline.
Try: “Show total revenue, unique customers, and average order value as KPI cards.”
explore.BarNote:
Aggregates a measure by category; bar length encodes value. Ideal for ranked comparisons and small multiples.
Try: “Show revenue by product category, sorted descending, with data labels on each bar.”
explore.HistogramNote:
Estimates a distribution by binning a continuous variable; reveals shape, skewness, and outliers. Choose bin count or use rules (e.g., Freedman–Diaconis).
Try: “Plot a histogram of exam scores with 30 bins and overlay a density curve.”
explore.BoxNote:
Summarizes distributions via median, quartiles (IQR), whiskers, and outliers. Great for group-wise comparison.
Try: “Show box plots of salary by department and mark outliers.”
explore.SectorNote:
Shows parts-of-a-whole by encoding proportion as slice angle. Best with few categories or a dominant share.
Try: “Create a pie chart of market share by vendor and slightly separate the largest slice.”
explore.ViolinNote:
Combines a kernel density estimate with a box plot summary, showing the full distribution shape alongside median, quartiles, and outliers. Wider sections indicate higher density.
Try: “Show violin plots of salary distribution by department with inner box plots.”
explore.CandleNote:
Displays open, high, low, close (OHLC) values per time period as candlestick bodies and wicks. Green/red coloring highlights upward/downward movement. Essential for financial time-series analysis.
Try: “Plot a candlestick chart of daily stock prices for the last 3 months.”
explore.ContourNote:
Draws iso-lines or filled regions connecting points of equal value on a 2D plane. Reveals gradients, peaks, and valleys in scalar fields. Similar to topographic maps.
Try: “Create a filled contour plot of elevation data over an X/Y grid.”
explore.MapNote:
Visualizes spatial data on interactive geographical maps. Multiple map types are available: heatmaps, scatter points, choropleths, hexagonal grids, and more. Under the hood, NVEIL leverages deck.gl for advanced geo rendering.
Try: “Display a choropleth map of population density by country.”
explore.SankeyNote:
Shows flows between stages with band widths proportional to quantities. Ideal for visualizing energy transfers, budget allocations, or conversion funnels.
Try: “Create a Sankey diagram of energy sources flowing to electricity, heat, and end consumers.”
explore.ParallelNote:
Plots each variable on a separate parallel vertical axis; each observation is a polyline connecting its values across all axes. Reveals correlations, clusters, and trade-offs in high-dimensional data.
Try: “Parallel coordinates plot of car specifications colored by fuel efficiency.”
explore.PartitionNote:
Represents hierarchical data as nested rectangles whose area encodes value. Drill down through parent–child relationships to see how parts compose the whole.
Try: “Build a treemap of company headcount broken down by department and team.”
explore.VectorFieldNote:
Visualizes direction and magnitude of 2D/3D vector data using arrows (quiver) and streamlines. Supports animated flow, clipping planes, and interactive probes in 3D. Color encodes magnitude via any palette, including medical presets.
Try: “Show the wind velocity field as arrows colored by speed, with streamlines.”

trust.localCurrency: Converted to your currency at checkout

changePlan.q: Can I change plans at any time?
changePlan.a: Yes, you can upgrade to a higher plan at any time. You will be asked to pay the difference between the two plans. If you wish to downgrade to a lower plan, you will need to cancel your subscription and wait until your current subscription ends.

payment.q: What payment methods do you accept?
payment.a: We accept all major credit cards (Visa, MasterCard, American Express) and can arrange invoicing for Enterprise plans.

trial.q: Is there a free trial for Pro?
trial.a: Yes! Pro comes with a 14-day free trial. No credit card required to start.

vizLimit.q: What happens when I reach my visualization limit?
vizLimit.a: On the Free plan, you'll be prompted to upgrade. Your existing visualizations remain accessible.

academic.q: Do you offer discounts for students or researchers?
academic.a: Yes, we offer special pricing for academic institutions. Contact us at info@nveil.com for details.

seo.homeTitle: NVEIL – No-Code AI Data Visualization | Auditable Insights
seo.homeDescription: Turn your data into production-ready visualizations in seconds. Reliable no-code AI with auditable, deterministic results — no hallucinations.
seo.exploreDescription: Explore interactive charts, 3D surfaces, maps, and more — generated by NVEIL's deterministic AI. No code required.
seo.feedbackDescription: Share feedback to help us improve NVEIL.
seo.planTitle: NVEIL Plans & Pricing | No-Code Data Visualization
seo.planDescription: Choose a flexible plan for AI-powered data visualization. Free tier included, upgrade anytime.
seo.dashboardDescription: View and manage your multi-panel dashboards.

step1.title: Describe What You See
step1.content: This is the chat panel. Describe what you want to visualize in plain language — bar charts, maps, scatter plots, 3D surfaces — and our AI will build it from your data.

stepUpload.title: Upload Your Data
stepUpload.content: Use this button to upload your data files (CSV, Excel, JSON). You can also drag and drop files directly into the chat area.

step25.title: Replay Visualizations
step25.content: Click these buttons in the chat history to reload any previously generated visualization. Each one is fully interactive.

stepExportDashboard.title: Export to Dashboard
stepExportDashboard.content: Use this button to send a visualization directly to a dashboard. Build rich multi-panel dashboards from your chat history.

step3.title: Interactive Rendering
step3.content: This is not a static image — it's a real interactive visualization. Rotate 3D surfaces, zoom into maps, hover for details, and export high-quality images.

step4.title: Discover Viz Types
step4.content: Browse all available visualization types and color palettes. Find inspiration for your next analysis.

stepDashboard.title: Build Dashboards
stepDashboard.content: Combine multiple visualizations into a dashboard. We've prepared one with the Airbnb data — check it out!

stepFinish.title: Ready to Explore
stepFinish.content: You're all set! The Airbnb dataset is pre-loaded with sample visualizations. Try clicking the viz buttons in the chat.

viz.sessionReloadWarning: Your visualization session will reload in {{seconds}} seconds

landing.heroSubtitle: Describe what you need in plain language. Get production-ready visualizations in seconds, fully auditable, no hallucinations.

ai.title: AI-Powered Analysis
ai.desc: Describe your visualization in natural language. NVEIL's AI interprets your data schema, selects the optimal chart type, and configures every encoding channel automatically.

auditable.title: Auditable & Deterministic
auditable.desc: Every visualization is generated through a transparent, reproducible pipeline. No black-box guessing — inspect every step from data transform to final render.

vizTypes.title: 30+ Visualization Types
vizTypes.desc: Bar charts, scatter plots, histograms, heatmaps, 3D surfaces, geographic maps, force-directed networks, Sankey flows, treemaps, parallel coordinates, and more.

upload.title: Upload Your Data
upload.desc: Drag and drop CSV, JSON, DICOM, MHD, ZRAW, and much more. NVEIL automatically detects columns, data types, and relationships.

describe.title: Describe What You Need
describe.desc: Ask for a specific chart or let the AI recommend the best visualization.

visualize.title: Get Production-Ready Visuals
visualize.desc: Interactive, exportable visualizations rendered instantly. Zoom, filter, and explore your data directly in the browser.

landing.exploreSubtitle: Bar charts, networks, 3D surfaces, geographic maps, Sankey flows, treemaps, and more — every type is fully tweakable.
landing.videoSubtitle: From raw data to polished visuals in seconds.

research.title: Scientific Research
research.desc: Molecular structures, spectral analysis, statistical distributions, and experimental data.

business.title: Business Analytics
business.desc: KPI dashboards, revenue forecasting, funnel analysis, and competitive benchmarking.

education.title: Education & Academia
education.desc: Research papers, student performance, curriculum analytics, and publication metrics.

geographic.title: Geographic Analysis
geographic.desc: Choropleth maps, spatial clustering, route optimization, and geospatial heatmaps.

engineering.title: Data Science & Engineering
engineering.desc: Exploratory data analysis, feature distributions, model diagnostics, pipeline metrics, and experiment tracking.

widgets.ExportRequiresLicense: Upgrade your license to use this format
widgets.VolumeRenderingNote: Volumetric rendering is a technique to display 3D scalar data (CT, MRI, simulations) by computing how light passes through the entire volume so that each voxel contributes color and opacity. It reveals internal structures without extracting surfaces (like most 3D visualizations), making it valuable in medical imaging and scientific visualization. Window level sets the center of the intensity range mapped to display (brightness), while window width defines the span around that center (contrast). Narrow width increases contrast to highlight subtle differences; wide width lowers contrast to show a broader range of tissues or materials.
widgets.PointNote: A scatter plot is a 2D visualization that displays individual data points on a Cartesian plane, where each axis represents a variable and the position of each point encodes the values of those variables, making it useful for identifying correlations, clusters, or outliers. A point cloud extends this concept into 3D space, where each point has three coordinates, often used in fields such as computer vision, geospatial analysis, or 3D scanning to represent the spatial distribution of objects or measurements. Variants include the bubble chart, which adds a third or fourth dimension by encoding values in the size or color of the points, and terms like XY plot, scattergram, or dot plot are also sometimes used depending on context.
widgets.HistogramNote: A histogram is a graphical representation of the distribution of a numerical dataset. The data range is divided into intervals of equal width, called bins, and each bin counts how many data points fall within its boundaries. The bins are plotted as adjacent rectangles, with the height (or area) of each bar proportional to the frequency of observations in that interval. By adjusting the number or width of bins, the histogram can reveal different levels of detail in the data distribution, helping to identify central tendency, spread, skewness, and outliers. It should not be confused with a bar plot, which represents categorical data with separated bars, while a histogram is specifically for continuous or numerical variables and uses contiguous bins.
widgets.LineNote: A line chart is a type of graph that displays information as a series of data points connected by straight line segments. It is commonly used to visualize trends over time or continuous data. Each point on the line represents a value at a specific time or category, and the connecting lines help to illustrate the overall direction and changes in the data. Line charts are particularly useful for showing how a variable evolves, making it easy to identify patterns, fluctuations, and correlations within the dataset.
widgets.BarNote: A bar chart is a graphical representation of data using rectangular bars, where the length of each bar is proportional to the value it represents. Bar charts are used to compare different categories or groups of data, making it easy to visualize differences and trends. They can be displayed vertically or horizontally and are commonly used in various fields such as business, economics, and social sciences to present categorical data clearly and effectively. Do not mistake a bar chart with a histogram, which represents the distribution of numerical data.
widgets.CandleNote: A candlestick chart, also known as an OHLC or Japanese candlestick chart, displays the open, high, low, and close prices of a financial instrument over a series of time periods. Each candle's body spans from the open to the close price, with the body filled or colored to indicate whether the close was higher (typically green) or lower (typically red) than the open. Thin lines extending above and below the body, called wicks or shadows, mark the highest and lowest prices reached during the period. Candlestick charts are widely used in financial and trading analysis to identify price patterns, momentum shifts, and potential reversals across daily, weekly, or intraday timeframes.
widgets.BoxViolinNote: A box plot (or box-and-whisker plot) is a standardized way of displaying the distribution of data based on a five-number summary: minimum, first quartile (Q1), median, third quartile (Q3), and maximum. The 'box' represents the interquartile range (IQR) between Q1 and Q3, with a line inside the box indicating the median. 'Whiskers' extend from the box to the minimum and maximum values within 1.5 times the IQR from the quartiles, while points outside this range are considered outliers and plotted individually. A violin plot combines aspects of a box plot with a kernel density estimate, providing a more detailed view of the data distribution. It displays the density of the data at different values, with the width of the 'violin' shape indicating the frequency of data points. This allows for a richer understanding of the data's distribution, including multimodal patterns that a box plot alone may not reveal.
widgets.SectorNoteTitle: Pie Chart or Circular Bar Chart
widgets.SectorNote: A pie chart is a circular statistical graphic that is divided into slices to illustrate numerical proportions. Each slice represents a category's contribution to the whole, with the size of each slice proportional to its percentage of the total. Pie charts are commonly used to visualize relative sizes of parts within a dataset, making it easy to compare different categories at a glance. However, they are most effective when there are a limited number of categories, as too many slices can make the chart difficult to read. A circular bar chart is a variation where bars are arranged in a circular layout, with the length of each bar representing the value of each category, providing a visually appealing alternative to traditional pie charts while still conveying proportional relationships.
widgets.SurfaceNote: Transforms scattered data points into a continuous surface, revealing underlying structures in either two or three dimensions. Instead of forcing data onto a grid like a heatmap, it uses a more sophisticated method called a Gaussian weighted average (Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression). For each original data point, a new smoothed value is calculated by averaging its neighbors, with closer points having a stronger influence. In both modes, the visualization is controlled by smoothing intensity and resolution (grid density). A low intensity makes the surface hug the original data points closely, preserving fine details. A high intensity averages over a larger neighborhood, producing a much smoother, more generalized surface that highlights the broader trend. In 3D, an additional step connects the resulting 3D points into a network of triangles (a process called Delaunay Triangulation). Ultimately, this transforms scattered, noisy measurements into clear, interpretable visuals—either as a 2D trend or a 3D landscape.
widgets.ContourNote: Contour lines are curves that connect points of equal value on a 2D surface or a 3D volume. They are commonly used in topographic maps to represent elevation, but can also depict other scalar fields such as temperature, pressure, or concentration. By following these lines, one can easily visualize gradients and patterns in the data, as well as identify peaks, valleys, and slopes. Contour lines help to simplify complex data by providing a clear and intuitive way to understand spatial variations across a given area.
widgets.UnigridNote: A heatmap (called unigrid in NVEIL) is a visualization that projects scalar values onto a regular 2D or 3D grid, with color intensity encoding the magnitude of each value. In the 2D case, cells appear as colored squares; in the 3D case, as colored voxels, both allowing users to detect gradients, clusters, and anomalies across dense datasets. When the input data is irregularly spaced, NVEIL interpolates values onto the uniform grid using a Gaussian kernel. This interpolation is controlled by three parameters: resolution (the density of the target grid), smoothing range (or the radius of the kernel: the spatial extent of influence around each data point) and smoothing intensity (the weighting strength applied by the kernel). This process ensures that scattered measurements are transformed into a smooth, continuous representation that remains consistent and comparable throughout the domain.
widgets.NodeNote: A network graph (or force-directed graph) visualizes relationships between entities as nodes connected by edges. It uses a physics simulation to position nodes, where connected nodes attract each other and unconnected nodes repel, revealing clusters, central hubs, and the overall topology of the network.
widgets.ChoroplethNote: A choropleth map is a type of thematic map where geographic regions are shaded or patterned according to a data variable. The color intensity or pattern represents the magnitude of a value for each region, making it easy to visualize how that variable differs across geographic areas. Choropleth maps are commonly used to display population density, election results, economic indicators, or any data that can be aggregated by administrative boundaries such as countries, states, or counties. They are particularly effective for showing regional patterns and spatial distributions at a glance.
widgets.SankeyNote: Shows flows between stages with band widths proportional to quantities. Ideal for visualizing energy transfers, budget allocations, or conversion funnels.
widgets.ParallelNote: Plots each variable on a separate parallel vertical axis; each observation is a polyline connecting its values across all axes. Reveals correlations, clusters, and trade-offs in high-dimensional data.
widgets.PartitionNote: Represents hierarchical data as nested rectangles whose area encodes value. Drill down through parent–child relationships to see how parts compose the whole.
widgets.ClickToAddProbe: Click on the plot to add probes.
widgets.VectorFieldNote: A vector field visualization assigns a directional vector (velocity, force, gradient, etc.) to each point in space. In 2D, vectors are displayed as color-coded arrows scaled by magnitude, with optional streamlines to trace flow paths. In 3D, vectors appear as arrows or cones positioned in space. Color intensity encodes the vector's magnitude, making it easy to identify regions of high or low activity. Manual sampling points can be added to read the exact components and magnitude of the vector at a chosen location.
widgets.ProbeSliderHint: Use the sliders to position the intersection point, then add a point.
